speaker: interviewer
text: --happened after you left for the labor camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. We're in the labor camp. It was a relief because at least we had a couple meals a day. And the, the only thing-- the only problem was that we had one very rough German guard who used to love beating up people. You had to stay out of his way. And then we worked on trucks-- on large trucks. Actually, I didn't do-- frankly, I didn't do that much. I was a young kid. But there were a lot of older, experienced auto mechanics and truck mechanics-- Jewish-- who really knew how to work. And we even had blacksmiths. We used to make springs for those trucks. And machine-- I did do work in machine shop. And, and then we, we used to cook our own meals because they used to give us supplies. And we had little stoves in the rooms that we used to-- that's how I learned to make stew over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you sleep?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we had-- we had bunks. There were, like, three or four men to a room in the bunks. So actually, it was a big improvement over the life in the ghetto at that time. And, and there were some men that used to go outside and smuggle in stuff, although it was very dangerous. And there was one man who used to-- who was a real bon vivant and used to go out and visit his girlfriends. And he thought he could do anything. But one time, they caught him. And they shot him, you know. So he pushed his luck too far. And they did it publicly so that we could all see what can happen, you know. And, and then, at one time, one of the men that ran away from camp-- he ran away from the camp. And we didn't know what happened to him. And he came back about six weeks later, smuggled himself back in into the camp. And he started telling us these stories that they picked him up in the ghetto, and they took him to Treblinka. And he told us that were-- they were exterminating Jews in Treblinka, whole trainloads full. They used to strip them naked. And they drove them into those chambers-- gas chambers. And then they-- he was one of the ones that used to take out the gold teeth and, and the jewelry, things like that. And that one time-- what he did is he packed-- he, he sneaked into a railroad car that was-- there were clothing. They were shipping back the clothing to Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he sneaked into that railroad car. And that's how he saved himself, you know. But when he came and he told us about it, you know, people wanted to beat him up. They thought he was crazy, that he's trying to stir up trouble, you know? What did you think?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody could believe about it. We were all aghast, you know. We just couldn't believe. I, I-- we did start believing a little bit. But mostly, we didn't believe him. And they called him the, the meshugganah-- you know, the crazy guy, you know. So that was really hard to comprehend that something like this could happen. And then-- so we were there for about a year. And then, at that time--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the food like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they used to give us potatoes and a portion of meat and some bread and some margarine. So it wasn't that bad, really. And then we used to-- we were able, somehow or other-- buy some food from the people. They used to smuggle in-- the food into the camp. So it wasn't that terrible. Of course, nobody got fat. But we didn't starve.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was a typical day like? What was the name of the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: The, the name of this camp was Luftwaffe Mokotow. And it was in an area of Warsaw called Praga, which was like in-- let's say-- like here, you would say North Hollywood, you know. It was about that distance from, from Warsaw, you know. And it was on the other side of the river-- the river Wisla-- Vistula. Well, they-- we used to get up in the morning. And we had to stay on the-- on the Platz, you know, where they used to, you know, count us over and over. And then the Gestapo man would come out with his whip and his dogs. And he would have a big speech, you know, how we have to behave ourselves, and, you know, you know, really reading the riot act to us. And then we would go to work for 8 hours or 10 hours. And then we would have a little free time to sit and talk, or whatever. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do at work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, mainly what I did, I did-- I used to run-- you know, clean the brake pads and clean the parts, you know, that were to be reused, wash them and clean them. Because I wasn't that experienced in the auto mechanics as some people were. So I, I was mainly-- as a helper. And sometimes, we had to do some digging if they wanted to build something, you know, some labor work. But it wasn't excruciating. It wasn't that bad as it was later on, you know. But this didn't last too long, you know. Because, like I said, it was about a year, and then they took us-- and they closed up this, this location. I don't know if they closed it completely. But I know that they took all the Jews, and they sent them back to the ghetto. But not to live in the ghetto-- they took us directly to-- there was a, a railroad station, Gdanska. It used to be called the Dworzec Gdanska. It means the Gdanska [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Station, you know, railroad station. And over there, there were a lot of Ukrainian [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] soldiers. And they drove us in a-- and, and they drove us mercilessly, you know, here and there and here, you know, pushed us around and, and beat us, you know, with rifle butts, and all that. And they took-- whoever had a watch or a ring had to surrender it to them, you know, by force. And, and that was the prelude to being shipped out to Auschwitz from there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was around 1942, I would say, '42. One thing I remember there was-- I met a young girl who was about my age-- dark-haired, beautiful girl. And she was so petrified, she was shaking all over. And she, she was so scared.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she, she asked me-- you know, we kind of got introduced. And she said, please take care of me. Help me, help me, help me. So I said, I'll do whatever I can, you know. What could I do, you know? But I guess she needed somebody just to lean on. Where did you meet her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right at this station-- railroad station. It was like an Umschlagplatz, where they transferred people to go to concentration camps. But then after a while, the thing-- after a while, when they drove us into the railroad cars, then I lost, lost sight of her, you know, because you had no choice where you went-- wherever they pushed you. And, and the railroad cars, they just had little windows on top, barred with barbed wire. There were maybe two windows on top. And they packed the whole car with people. So it was unbelievably hot and stuffy there. You couldn't hardly breathe.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people?

speaker: interviewee
text: God, [SIGHS] hundreds of people they shoved in a wagon, you know, cattle-- like cattle cars, you know. But they were not open. It would be good if they were open. But they were closed. And I remember, when we took off, the only nice thing about it-- you could hear the railroad whistles, you know, you know, this-- the-- which was the only pleasant thing you could hear, is the railroad whistle. And then, during the night, I lost my-- I don't know whether it was at night or in the daytime-- I think we went like two days and two nights. And I lost consciousness. And what I remember, to this day-- the dream that I had, like, that I was laying on a field of green grass, with a river or a brook. To this day, I remember that. I guess I was dreaming of something that I would like to happen to me. But it didn't happen. Then, I-- when, when-- finally, when I woke up-- I must have passed out, you know. I woke up. And they opened the doors. And they started, los, los, los, pulling everybody out, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: The guard-- the Nazis, you know, the SS, with the-- with the skull, skull, skull-and-bones [POINTS] emblem on their hats, you know, and on their-- and their-- and on their epaulettes. And a lot of people never came out of those wagons. They were dead. Because they were laying like layers on top of layers, you know. Well, I was young and strong. And I think that's why I survived. You know, a lot of people didn't. And as they unloaded us, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there was Majdanek-- Lublin Majdanek. And they-- I'll never forget, you know. Whoever couldn't make it, they shot them. And in my eyes, they shot-- there was an old lady. She was maybe in her 70s. Or maybe, in a sense-- you know, in those days, people looked older, you know. Maybe in her-- she looks very-- she could hardly walk, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So an SS man pulled out a gun and blew her-- hit her in the-- shot her in the head. And I could see the brain splatter all over, you know. I'll never forget to this day.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so that was Majdanek. And they, they marched us to Majdanek. Majdanek was like a death camp, you know. Very few people survived Majdanek. It was mainly beatings and, and work in the sand and the gravel and digging. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And there were a lot of Ukrainian guards and Polack-- Polish guards who used to beat the, the prisoners. They called them [NON-ENGLISH]. And they used to stand-- you know, if they wanted to kill somebody, they used to put a board across his neck and just rock on his neck until he died, you know. It was mostly Ukrainian and Polish guards there. But luckily, I wasn't that long over there. It was only about six month, I think. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And from then--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you sleep while you were in Majdanek?

speaker: interviewee
text: They had those barracks [SIGHS] that had, like, layers of wooden platforms-- like shoebox-- like a shoebox, you know. And you were laying on wood, you know, basically. There was no-- nothing under you, just boards, you know. And that was the-- that's how we used to sleep. And the food was very, very little. There was very little food.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Soup, which was usually mostly water and cabbage, maybe a little pork in it, very little, maybe a few little potatoes swimming-- and a piece of bread a day. That's about all it was over there. A lot of people died there constantly. It was a--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do during the day?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was also a crematorium there, too. Oh, mostly, they used to drive us out and told us-- it was just useless work, you know. Dig here, or dig there. And there was nothing really creative to do. It was mostly just digging and loading and digging and loading. There was nothing creative over there. It was just mostly like a death camp, you know. But I guess I was lucky because there wasn't-- I was-- like I say, I wasn't there that long, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you wearing in Majdanek?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Majdanek, as far as I remember, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you know, there were, like, cotton-- with stripes-- clothes, with a little round hat, also with stripes. You know, like prisoners used to wear in the olden days here, you know-- cotton. It was summer at that time, I remember, so it wasn't cold.

speaker: interviewer
text: Does any incident stand in your mind from your time in Majdanek?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there was only, only one incident that stands in my [LAUGHS] mind. Before I left-- you know, when we-- when we went to the ghetto-- it comes back to me now-- I did see my mother for a short time, you know. And she gave me a gold coin that she saved, a $20 gold coin. So I had it with me. But when I went to the Majdanek, there was nothing to do with the gold coin, you know. So I had a relative there, an older-- I was a kid, you know. I had an older relative. And he says, if you give me the gold coin, I will see that you have food. I'll buy it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I gave him the gold coin. But I never saw anything. [LAUGHS] He just took it, you know. So people were mostly-- well, this particular person, you know, was-- now that I look-- but even before the war, a con man, you know. So I entrusted him because I knew him, you know. And I never saw-- I don't want to mention the name because his relatives are still alive. So no use bringing it up. So that's what stands out.

speaker: interviewee
text: But also what steps up in my mind is, like, when they drove us to work, watching the guards torturing the prisoners. You know, like lay-- like I said, laying a 2' by 4' across the neck, and, and a rock on the throat, you know. But this, this is probably what stands out in my own mind-- and always trying to be someplace else where the guard was-- you know, trying to avoid, yeah. And the speeches that they gave us, the Polish-- the, the, the, the guards-- the Polish guards, that, that we'll never survive this camp. We better get used to the idea that this is the end of the line, you know-- in Polish-- in the Polish language, you know-- that this is-- that we'll never get out alive from there. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What else-- What happened next?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, what happened next-- that they loaded us up into, into railroad cars again. And they took us to Auschwitz. And when we went into Auschwitz-- you probably saw that well-publicized gate, Arbeit macht frei, you know. I saw that gate. I went through that gate. They drove us through it. And they made us strip completely naked and hold our hands to show that we have all our fingers. And then Dr. Mengele was standing there.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know he was Dr. Mengele?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I didn't know him at that time. But from what I hear, the descriptions now-- there was a young, tall, good-looking, handsome guy, brunette, you know. So it must have been him, you know. And he-- we used to run back and forth, men and women, naked, you know. And he would tell you, go this way or this way, this way or this way. And, you know, I was-- like I said, I was athletic. And I was young. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say probably 18 or 19. So I was sent to go to a labor-- to the-- to the labor camp out of Auschwitz, which was called Buna Werke. And my mother was sent-- I saw my mother there, too. My mother-- I saw she was also in an area closed off, you know. But I haven't seen her afterwards.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was sent to this-- called Buna Werke, which worked for a German-- big German concern called IG Farbenindustrie, which was making ersatz gasoline, you know, and-- artificial gasoline and artificial rubber, and other products like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And because I was a machinist-- you know, I told them, you know, when they asked me-- they sent me to a, a machine, machine Kommando. So that I-- you know, so that I would-- but actually, what it was, we didn't really do any machinist type of work. What we did is helping German [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] structural technicians and engineers who were, you know, setting up electrical, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] high-powered transformers, and things like that, you know. And so we would carry the transformers for them. And we would carry the acetylene bottles, you know, for welding, and things like that. And so that wasn't quite as bad as people that had to work in, in, in outdoor, heavy work. And-- but unfortunately, that didn't last. Because an incident happened that almost, you know, ended my life there. Myself and another man were carrying a heavy transformer, which was about 5 feet long and very heavy. It must have weighed 120 pounds or something. We were carrying it up on a beam-- on a narrow beam. I was holding one end. And he was holding another. And all of a sudden, it slipped out of his hand. And I couldn't hold it myself. So it crashed to the floor. And, and when the German kapo said, [GERMAN], you know, what happened, he pointed at me. He says that I dropped it. I said, no, I didn't drop it. But because he was the one that initiated it, you know-- so they believed him. And they kicked me out. And they sent me to the-- what they called the Toten Kommando, or the Death Kommando, which was working all day, loading heavy lorries, they called them, you know. They were those wagons, you know, railroad cars that kind of tilted sideways to dump-- to dump out the dirt and tilted upright to load them up. And we had to work all day in the hot sun with very little food, you know. And when you work hard, you need more food. So a lot of people were just dying there, you know, getting too weak. And when you got too weak, they sent you to the crematorium. So I felt the life ebbing out of me, you know, that I would not survive. After about three days or four days, I said, I'm not going to make it anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I took courage. And the, the, the Lageralteste, who was the head of the whole-- of the whole camp, of Buna Werke, was a big German. And he had a green triangle, which indicated that he was a [GERMAN], which means a criminal. But he, he was the head of the whole camp. He was big, about 6 feet 4 or something, like, big, giant man.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so on Sunday, when we didn't work, I took courage. And I went, and I walked up to him. And I stood upright and saluted him. And I told him in German that I would like to have a few words to tell him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he let me say. And I told him the whole story, you know, what happened, that I am, by training, a, you know, a Maschine Schlosser and that I'm dying there in this. And what purpose do I serve? I said, at least, if I work at my profession, I will serve some purpose for Germany, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he took down my number, you know, which is 127915. And he wrote it down. And the next day, they called out my name. And they sent me out to another Kommando, which was also a technical Kommando.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's what saved my life. Because I spoke up, because I had the courage to walk up to him. Because everybody was terrified of him, you know. He used to give people a [GERMAN], you know, and, and knock him down, you know. But I guess it worked [LAUGHS] for me. So then I was sent to another Kommando, which was a lot better. We used to transport acetylene bottles and the oxygen bottles, which they use in welding. And so that was a lot easier. And I worked closer with some German technicians-- craftsmen. Do you remember any of their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't remember. I remember there was one man who used to bring me a sandwich almost every day. He was a German. He was one of the craftsmen. But I guess he had a heart. So he used to bring me a sandwich every-- he used to share his lunch with me every day. So that-- I guess that was a bright, bright side of it, you know. And, and that lasted until the Germans started-- I mean, the Russians-- the Russians started getting closer. And we could-- we could hear the artillery booming. And also, the planes were dropping bombs all around at Buna. And we were hoping it would drop on the Germans. Unfortunately, I think they missed. But they did damage the factories, you know, and the installations.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say, probably 1994, 1994.

speaker: interviewee
text: 1944. I'm sorry, [LAUGHS] I said '94-- wishful thinking. No, it was 1944, yes. And the Germans were coming in closer and closer. So they evacuated Auschwitz. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they had us-- they gave each one of us a ration of bread for-- to go on the road. So I sewed myself up a little sac that I-- it was hanging from my neck inside my shirt. Because people would steal from another, you know. Anybody would steal. And I don't say 'anybody,' but many people would to save their life.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of clothes were you wearing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Incidentally, going back to Auschwitz, as I remember now-- we were talking about it before. They used to-- they used to bring Jewish people from all over Europe. And the characterization-- like, we talked about Jewish people from Italy. They had it the hardest over there because they were completely unused to the hardships. And the second group of Jews that had it very bad were the Dutch Jews from Holland. They were all very well educated, very refined people, very nice people. Unfortunately, there was no demand there for refined and nice people. It was dog-eat-dog, you know. So they didn't last very well, either. And then the, the Hungarian Jews came in. And Hungarian Jews only spoke Hungarian. They didn't speak any Jewish at all. Consequently, they didn't make any friends with the other Jewish people. And myself, I learned to speak Yiddish in the labor camp. And as I stayed on longer and longer, my-- I perfected my Yiddish. Because you had to, to get along with people. And so that's what I wanted to bring up-- it came to my mind, you know, how different Jewish people from different parts of Europe were better equipped for this hard, hard life. Ourselves, being from Poland, we always had hardship from the Polacks. So we were used, a little better, to the hard life. Also, many Jews from Poland-- from Poland did work as craftsmen and did work as-- they had-- they were oriented into the physical work more than the Jews from Italy or Holland or Belgium, or other fields like that. The Jews from Belgium did speak Yiddish, most of them, you know. Because I guess their ancestors came from Eastern Europe.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you asked me what we were wearing. OK, here's, here's something good to bring up. We were wearing-- summer and winter, it was just a, a blouse, you know, a striped blouse. I don't if it was cotton, or ersatz-- artificial cotton, I don't know, and, and pants, and underwear, you know, sort of underwear.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- which in the summertime wasn't too bad, when it was hot. But in wintertime, when we had to work, it was freezing cold. And in the wintertime, as I remember, they did give us a little cotton overcoat, which also wasn't very warm. And we had a hat, a round hat, with the stripes. And so what we were doing, we were taking-- we had contact with cement because we had to load cement where-- railroad cars and unload cement-- railroad cars with cement. So we used to take the empty bags from cement. And I used to cut out-- we used to cut out holes for the arms and for the neck. And we would slip it-- shake it out good and slip it under our clothing. And also, I would take the cement-- the bag-- and cut it out in strips and wrap up our legs and our torso, you know, to keep the wind out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did it help?

speaker: interviewee
text: It helped a lot. And it insulated us. But the Germans were against it. And if they caught somebody, you would get a whipping, a beating. But luckily, I wasn't caught. So all winter, I was wearing cement-- I mean, cement-bag underwear up and down. You, you almost did everything you could to survive. Sometimes it was the difference whether you stood in a windy place or went into a place that wasn't windy, you know. It was that much difference when you're weak and tired and hungry, and all that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to your shoes? What kind of shoes were you wearing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that's another problem-- the shoes. They were killers. They were [GERMAN], they use to call them. They were-- they had wooden soles and leather-- just leather thongs on top-- strips on top, you know, to hold them. Or there-- they were all, you know, like, closed on top, you know. And-- but it was wooden, wooden soles. And it was a torture to walk in them, you know. And I think that's what ruined my legs because I had varicose veins and-- afterwards, after the liberation, you know. But that, that was a torture, walking in those shoes-- the wooden soles.
